title: Juice on Carpet
stainChemistry: Fruit juice contains anthocyanins or carotenoids (depending on fruit), citric acid, fructose, and tannins. On carpet, the pigment bonds through hydrogen bonding, and the sugar component creates a sticky matrix that traps the dye against the fiber. Oxidation over time deepens the color and makes the stain increasingly resistant to treatment.
whyThisWorks: The acidic tannin spotter breaks pigment-fiber bonds on carpet. Hydrogen peroxide oxidizes the chromophore structures responsible for color. Quick treatment is key — fresh juice stains are water-soluble, but dried stains require significantly more effort as the sugars crystallize and tannins cross-link.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden spot
homeSolutions.instruction: Find a hidden patch of the same carpet — inside a closet, under furniture, behind a door. Dab a drop of the Dawn + 3% peroxide solution from step 5 and blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the carpet holds its color, you're safe. If dye transfers or the fiber lightens, skip to step 9 — home peroxide will bleach the carpet dye.

homeSolutions.agent: Identify the juice — artificial-dye juices need more patience
homeSolutions.instruction: Juice color comes from one of three things: natural anthocyanin (grape, cranberry, pomegranate), natural carotenoid (orange, mango, carrot), or ADDED Red #40 / Blue #1 food dye (most kid juice boxes, sports drinks, Gatorade, Kool-Aid). Artificial dyes are engineered to resist water — they often need 2–3x the cycles, plus a sunlight finish. Treat the same way, just plan for more rounds.

Juice is a tannin stain plus a LOT of sugar. Two rules: (1) no alkaline cleaners (ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean on untreated tannin) — they lock the color in, and (2) don't skip step 4. The sugar in juice caramelizes as it dries into a sticky film that attracts dirt and makes the stain bigger over weeks. Cold water flush removes the sugar before chemistry starts.

Press a folded white cloth or paper-towel stack straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from outside in. After blotting, pour a small amount of cold water on the spot and blot again. Repeat 2–3 times. You're flushing sugar and fresh pigment out before the real work starts. Don't flood — water that reaches the pad causes wicking rings.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the stain — outside edges in. Let it sit 5–10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes natural tannin pigments and gradually breaks down artificial food dyes. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Expect 2–3 cycles for Red #40 and Blue #1.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On dark carpet, skip this step if the patch test showed any dye transfer.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dab onto the area with a clean white cloth. The mild acid neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps anthocyanin pigments in their lighter (red) form rather than drifting toward blue-purple. Blot dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry down — stop pad wicking overnight
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack six to ten sheets of white paper towels over the spot, weigh them down with a heavy book, and leave overnight. This pulls any residual juice up out of the pad before it can wick back as a ring. This step matters more for juice than for most stains because of the sugar — if any is left in the pad, it will keep attracting stains in the same spot.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight inspect — sunlight finish for artificial dye
homeSolutions.instruction: Inspect in natural light. If a pale shadow remains and the juice was an artificial-dye product (sports drink, kids' juice), consider opening blinds and letting sunlight fall on the spot for a few hours over the next day. UV naturally breaks down Red #40. If it's a natural juice (grape, cranberry), repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two full rounds is the home ceiling.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test fails, the carpet is wool, or the stain survives two rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "[Type] juice on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No alkaline products used. Suspected artificial Red/Blue dye / natural anthocyanin." That handoff saves them 20 minutes of diagnosing pigment type.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If juice stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is juice on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
